text: We investigate by means of first-principle calculations within the deformation potential theory the intrinsic carrier mobility of monolayer sheets and nanoribbons of MoS2. Unlike graphene, where the mobility is reduced by almost three orders of magnitude when going from a 2D sheet to nanoribbons, the carrier mobility in armchair nanoribbons of MoS2 is almost the same as that of 2D sheets and barely depends on the width of the ribbon. Moreover, a room temperature transport polarity reversal is observed: the 2D sheet sheet is hole dominant (μh = 200.52 cm2 V−1 s−1 and μe = 72.16 cm2 V−1 s−1) whereas the 4 nm wide nanoribbon is electron dominant (μe = 190.89 cm2 V−1 s−1 and μh = 49.72 cm2 V−1 s−1). The reason for the persistence of the mobility as well as the polarity reversal are related to the particular symmetry and distribution of the edge states present in MoS2 nanostructures. Hence, lateral confinement combined with edge engineering can be an effective way to modulate the transport properties of MoS2 nanoelectronics.
